title: Optimizing strong light-matter coupling of plasmonic lattices and monolayer semiconductors
authors: Lukas Krelle, Lukas Husel, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Ismail Bilgin, Alexander Högele, Farsane Tabataba-Vakili
abstract: Exciton-polaritons provide a versatile platform for the study of a wide range of phenomena, including polariton lasers, topological polaritons, and bosonic condensation. Transition metal dichalcogenide monolayers host excitons with large oscillator strength and binding energies constituting a robust matter constituent that forms polaritons from cryogenic to room temperature when embedded in optical microcavities. Plasmonic nanoparticles arranged in lattice geometries offer strong field-confinement and high quality factors. However, the high sensitivity of monolayer excitons to strain and dielectric disorder necessitates encapsulation in atomically flat hBN to ensure a high optical quality, rendering plasmonics more challenging. Here, we employ our recently developed fabrication method for embedding gold nanodisk arrays into van der Waals heterostructures and compare two samples with opposite layer order. We observe that strain and etching-induced surface contamination can reduce the exciton quality and thus the light-matter interaction strength significantly. Our fabrication approach reduces interfacial irregularities and enables homogeneous large-area polariton lattices for a wide range of applications, such as polarization-control or topological polaritonics.
summary: The study demonstrates that the spatial arrangement of plasmonic nanodisks relative to a 2D semiconductor layer is critical for maintaining high-quality excitons. By placing the lattice on top of the semiconductor, the researchers reduced strain and disorder, leading to a 25% increase in light-matter coupling strength. This approach enables the creation of more homogeneous and robust polaritonic platforms.
main_problem: Optimizing the strength of light-matter coupling in plasmonic lattices integrated with monolayer semiconductors by addressing strain and dielectric disorder caused by fabrication processes.
main_result: Placing the plasmonic lattice on top of the semiconductor stack (Sample A) achieves a 25% increase in coupling strength and significantly improved exciton quality compared to a bottom-layer configuration.
method: The researchers used electron-beam lithography, plasma etching, and gold evaporation to embed gold nanodisk arrays into hBN/MoSe2 heterostructures, followed by momentum-resolved differential reflectance and photoluminescence spectroscopy.
model_or_system: The system consists of gold nanodisk arrays embedded in hexagonal Boron Nitride (hBN) layers coupled to MoSe2 monolayers, modeled using a coupled oscillator Hamiltonian to describe exciton-polariton formation.
key_observables: Exciton linewidth, exciton energy, spatial homogeneity (energy and linewidth), coupling strength (g), Rabi splitting, and Surface Lattice Resonance (SLR) Q-factors.
important_parameters: Exciton energy (~1.64 eV), coupling strength (23.5 meV in optimized sample), and exciton linewidth (3.0 meV in optimized sample).
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the primary degradation in the control sample is due to fabrication-induced strain and surface contamination/doping.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the fabrication sequence and a schematic comparison of the two sample geometries (top-lattice vs. bottom-lattice).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of light-matter coupling in 2D materials, describes the fabrication of two different layer-order heterostructures, presents spectroscopic characterization of exciton quality and coupling strength, and concludes with the implications for large-area polariton lattices.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and cavity QED as it demonstrates a practical method to maximize light-matter interaction strength and maintain high-quality resonances in hybrid plasmonic-semiconductor architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Fabrication of gold nanodisk arrays embedded in hBN. Fabrication sequence: (a) mechanical exfoliation of an hBN flake on a SiO2/Si substrate, (b) hBN flake covered with a PMMA film, (c) electron-beam lithography, (d) ICP-RIE process of the lattice, (e) gold deposition and (f) removal of the PMMA film. (g) Schematics of the two sample geometries. (h) Optical micrograph of sample A.

FIG. 2. Monolayer characteristics. (a) Representative PL and (b) Kramers-Kronig phase-corrected DR spectra of sample A with the linewidth indicated by black arrows. In inset in (a) indicates the sample geometry. (c) Distribution of the exciton linewidth and (d) resonance energy in an isolated MoSe2 region on sample A with dashed lines indicating a Gaussian distribution of the resonance energy and arrows marking the standard deviation. (e)-(h) Same as (a)-(d) but for sample B.

FIG. 3. Exciton-plasmon coupling characteristics. (a) Representative momentum-resolved polariton dispersion of sample A in DR with solid lines highlighting the fitted polariton branches and dashed lines indicating the exciton and SLR resonance energies. (b) Distribution of the resonance energy of the A1s exciton and (c) the SLR at k = 0 in sample A with dashed lines indicating a Gaussian distribution and the standard deviation marked by arrows. (d) Distribution of the exciton-plasmon coupling strength in sample A. (e)-(h) Same as (a)-(d) but for sample B.
